paragraphs.body: Is today, 3/14/2012? Am I seven years old? Why do I love volleyball so much? Why does it make me so heppy, full of power. Why do you all the time tell me to take care of school, because without school I can’t do anything? Why all the time do you stay next to me, allways looking at me closely, to don’t make the wrong move? Why do you care so much about getting BETTER?! I really don’t understand, why you put me all the time to “split” myself between volleyball and schoool? Will you ever have enough trust in me?

Dear dairy, Is today, 7/18/2020? Am I fifteen years old? Why do you treat me like I’m still a kid? Why all the time you tell me what to do when I already know what I want? Why do I feel like no fucking one gets me? Like, fr now, can you STOP to ask me all the time where I am, with who, and why? Don’t you have a life? Why, all the time, after practice you take my phone just because I didn’t do my homework? WHO CARES? WHY IT FEELS LIKE EVERTHING THAT I DO IS WRONG? Even in the practice, why he started to scream at me without reason? Like, why he doesn’t scream at the new girl, that I’M SORRY, but doesn’t know how to spike that motherfucking ball?

annotations.text: Even in the practice, why he started to scream at me without reason? Like, why he doesn’t scream at the new girl, that I’M SORRY, but doesn’t know how to spike that motherfucking ball?
annotations.comment: This is the only moment of this essay that feels as if it is not written to be read by your mother. Here it feels as though you are responding to all the adults in your life. This isn't necessarily bad, but if it happens here, you might consider having it happen throughout. Or changing the language of it a little bit to help us understand that you are asking this of your mom.
annotations.context_left: LIKE EVERTHING THAT I DO IS WRONG?

paragraphs.body: Can someone make me to understand... why adults think that I’m a puppet that they can control as they want? At this point, this is how life will be? All the time, full of fights with people around me? Why you all the time make comparations between me and my classmates? Do you think I’m not angry that everything you make me to think that is a competition? And if really it is... what I will get in the end? With what purpose I fight? Why do I use all my energy for a fucking sport that I do just because you don’t let me to give up? WHY CAN’T I HAVE A NORMAL LIFE????????????

Dear dairy, Is today, 5/16/2035? Am I a thirty year old? Today it was a good day, then why do I feel so nostalgic and full of thoughts? At work, we just finished to send the big project for the Fashion Week that will take place, here in NYC, but will Dior accept the chromatic palette? Even when I talked with Maria Grazia Chiuri, she was amazed by my ideas, but in the end, will the full project be a good one? Why am I so frustrated?

annotations.text: At work, we just finished to send the big project for the Fashion Week that will take place, here in NYC, but will Dior accept the chromatic palette? Even when I talked with Maria Grazia Chiuri, she was amazed by my ideas, but in the end, will the full project be a good one?
annotations.comment: This is one of the most concrete and specific passages in the entire piece. It's very interesting to me that it takes place in the future.

paragraphs.body: Today, I talked to you on the phone, and I really question myself, why were you so quiet? Do you think that I don’t know what you want to talk with me? It’s about kids? It’s about Eric? Do you really want to know why he is still my boyfriend, and not my husband? Do you ever wonder, why I don't want to discuss about it? Did you ever think, maybe I feel uncomfortable, or I don’t know the answers? When I came home, I told you I was overwhelmed with work and confused about what Eric wanted from me, not knowing what to do — do you still believe that “I’m too lazy” to find a solution?

After I left my volleyball career, you stated to behave with me like I did something wrong — did I? If I was player right now, do you think that our relationship will look different now? The one that knows the answer is you, right? Or is just a pressure that you put it on my shoulders to be better than you? I look back on time when I was walking with my dog, Hugo, that is like a kid for you — then a question got my mind: why do you want me to play volleyball? To be honest, I am really grateful for it, but do you still stick with that image when it comes to see me in your mind? I believe even now that you do this to me just to get used to the pressure of the life, of the idea that you need to lose something to win, but was it worth it?

Today I have in plan to meet with Stefi for dinner, but why do I have a feeling that she wants to tell me something important? I vividly commemorate the time when we made the decision to move in NYC, did you was happy or pound? I know that I saw that on your face, even if in your eyes it was something else, am I right? Did I saw the fear of being left alone? Simulations, I’m convinced that this feeling will hunt me too, but who knows?

Dear diary, Is today, 2/18/2085? Am I eighty years old? Is it my birthday today? Here I’m, thinking about you, as always, right? My kids, Hope and Dante, and my husband, Eric, hugged me so much.... do you think is something wrong with them? I really miss you, but do you miss me? I know that from up there you look at me and try to guide me as you always did, but did I was a good daughter? Now, reflecting on my past, my volleyball career, my designer one, and even of the job to be a mother, do you think I did a good job? Maybe, if I was more grateful in my teen years, do you think our relationship could be a closer one? I understand now, the pressure that you tried to get me use to, from the beginning- do you think my kids got used to it? I really tried my best, but was it enough? Do you think they see a part of you in me?

annotations.comment: Hello, Clearly, the central focusing idea of this piece is the relationship between you and your mother. You do a great job of staying centered on that by building the essay with your big ideas—your experiences with volleyball, your future career, and, of course, your conversations with your mother. There are two words that stand out the most to me, and, despite the fact that they are totally unrelated to each other, they seem to be very important to this essay: why and today. First off, this diary format is working very well for this conversation, and including today at the beginning of each passage really does reinforce the idea that this writing is taking place over a great deal of time which is a really interesting approach. The word why is very important in this piece, because so much of it is an attempt to get to know your mother better. Asking why questions of someone else is a great way to understand their perspective better, to get a better understanding of who they are or what they’ve done. Great communicators are extremely good at learning to ask such exploratory questions. What I admire most about this piece is that it truly does seem interested in the answers to the questions. There’s an old saying, “Every question possesses a power that does not lie in the answer,” and I believe your questions do have such powers, but, at the same time, you are not using that power as a source of guilt, blaming, or manipulating. While this document does clearly imitate traditional diary entries, the audience of the piece does not seem to be a future you, so much as your own mother, and that’s working very well. But there is also a way in which this essay could have a much broader audience of anybody struggling to understand a loved one better. You are illustrating your past, present, near future, and distant future in a way that might encourage a reader to think of their own life in this way. Perhaps, the most important thing I learned from this piece is that time works its way through us in this continuous manner—I see in this writing that you are still the person you were when you were seven, and you are currently the person you will be when you’re eighty. It’s really cool to see this. It makes me think of how some Native American languages don’t have past or future tenses. They say, “I go to the mountain today. I go to the mountain yesterday. I go to the mountain tomorrow.” This is because time is irrelevant to them. Everything is happening now. Everything has already happened. I’m oversimplifying the concept, but it came to mind reading your piece. It was a real pleasure reading your life in this way. Best of luck at Fashion Week! Thanks,
